event: Pre-ceramic Archaic (Ortoiroid) peoples, likely migrating from South America, established the earliest known settlements on the islands.
Saladoid Arawak-speaking peoples arrived from the Orinoco region, bringing pottery, agriculture, and permanent village life.
Taino culture developed on the islands, leaving behind petroglyphs, ball courts, and settlements such as those later found at Salt River on St. Croix.
Carib (Kalinago) peoples expanded into the region, increasingly contesting Taino communities in the Lesser Antilles.
Christopher Columbus, on his second voyage, landed at Salt River Bay on St. Croix, the only documented Columbus landing on present U.S. soil, sparking a violent clash with the Caribs.
Emperor Charles V claimed the islands for Spain, and subsequent decades saw indigenous populations decimated by warfare, enslavement, and European disease.
English and Dutch settlers established rival colonies on St. Croix, beginning a long contest among European powers for control of the islands.
Spanish forces expelled the English and Dutch from St. Croix, but the island soon passed to French control.
The Danish West India Company began colonizing St. Thomas, founding the settlement that became Charlotte Amalie.
Denmark claimed St. John, though active settlement of the island was delayed by disputes with neighboring colonial powers.
Denmark leased St. Thomas to the Brandenburg African Company, making the island a major hub of the transatlantic slave trade.
Danish planters formally settled St. John and began clearing land for sugar plantations worked by enslaved Africans.
Denmark purchased St. Croix from France, completing its acquisition of the three main islands.
Enslaved Akwamu Africans led a major slave rebellion on St. John, seizing control of much of the island for several months before being suppressed with French help.
The Danish crown took over direct administration of the islands from the bankrupt Danish West India Company, establishing the Danish West Indies as a royal colony.
St. Thomas was declared a free port, fueling its growth as a major Caribbean commercial and shipping center.
Denmark became the first nation to legislate the abolition of the transatlantic slave trade, with the ban taking effect in 1803.
Britain briefly occupied the Danish West Indies during the Napoleonic Wars before returning them to Denmark.
British forces again occupied the islands, holding them until they were restored to Denmark in 1815.
Sugar production and the plantation economy reached their peak before long-term decline driven by falling prices and exhausted soils.
Peter von Scholten became Governor-General, later pursuing reforms that improved conditions for the enslaved population.
Enslaved Africans on St. Croix, led by Moses 'General Buddhoe' Gottlieb, staged a mass uprising that compelled Governor von Scholten to declare immediate emancipation.
Labor laws restricting freed workers' mobility entrenched harsh conditions on plantations despite formal emancipation.
The United States and Denmark negotiated a treaty to sell the islands, but the U.S. Senate failed to ratify it.
The 'Fireburn' labor revolt erupted on St. Croix, led by women including Queen Mary, as workers burned plantations and the town of Frederiksted to protest oppressive labor contracts.
A second proposed treaty to sell the islands to the United States was approved by the U.S. Senate but rejected by the Danish parliament.
Denmark and the United States signed the Treaty of the Danish West Indies, agreeing to a sale for $25 million in gold, ratified by a Danish referendum.
The United States formally took possession of the islands on Transfer Day, March 31, renaming them the United States Virgin Islands amid World War I strategic concerns about German U-boats.
The U.S. Navy was placed in charge of administering the islands, governing them as a strategic naval outpost.
U.S. citizenship was granted to most residents of the Virgin Islands by act of Congress.
Administration of the islands was transferred from the Navy to the Department of the Interior, and the first civilian governor was appointed.
The first Organic Act established a civil government framework and limited home rule for the territory.
World War II brought expanded U.S. military installations, including a submarine base and airfields, boosting the local economy.
The Revised Organic Act reorganized the territorial government, creating a unicameral legislature and modernizing the islands' administration.
Laurance Rockefeller donated extensive lands on St. John to establish Virgin Islands National Park, protecting much of the island.
The Hess Oil refinery opened on St. Croix, becoming one of the world's largest and a major pillar of the island's economy.
Congress granted Virgin Islanders the right to elect their own governor, ending the era of presidentially appointed governors.
Melvin Evans took office as the first popularly elected Governor of the U.S. Virgin Islands.
Virgin Islanders gained the right to elect a non-voting Delegate to the U.S. House of Representatives.
Voters rejected a proposed territorial constitution, the latest of several failed efforts to establish local self-governance under a homegrown charter.
Hurricane Hugo devastated the islands, causing widespread destruction, looting, and the temporary deployment of federal troops to St. Croix.
Hurricane Marilyn struck St. Thomas, destroying much of its infrastructure and housing and severely damaging the tourism economy.
The HOVENSA oil refinery on St. Croix closed, eliminating thousands of jobs and dealing a major blow to the territory's economy.
Centennial commemorations marked 100 years of U.S. ownership, accompanied by ongoing debate over the islands' political status and Danish colonial legacy.
Hurricanes Irma and Maria struck the islands within weeks, causing catastrophic damage across St. Thomas, St. John, and St. Croix.
Albert Bryan was reelected Governor, leading recovery and infrastructure rebuilding efforts following the 2017 hurricanes.
